# Research: public-entry-rate-limiting-abuse-prevention

*Auto-generated by AIDLC planning session*

**Question:** What are effective rate limiting and anti-spam strategies for unauthenticated public entry submission endpoints in Node.js? What libraries (e.g. express-rate-limit, upstash/ratelimit) are production-ready, and what signals (IP, email, fingerprint) should be used?

---

# Rate Limiting & Anti-Spam for Public Entry Submission Endpoints (Node.js)

## Overview

Unauthenticated public entry endpoints (contest entries, waitlist signups, poll votes, etc.) are high-value abuse targets. Without authentication, traditional user-based rate limiting doesn't apply — you must rely on signals from the request itself. This document covers production-ready libraries, signal strategies, layered defenses, and concrete implementation patterns.

---

## Signal Inventory

### 1. IP Address
- **Pros:** Zero friction, always available, effective against unsophisticated bots
- **Cons:** Shared NAT (office/school networks), IPv6 rotation, residential proxies, VPNs
- **Best for:** First layer of defense; block obvious mass submitters
- **Caveat:** Never use as the *only* signal. A `X-Forwarded-For` header is trivially spoofed unless your reverse proxy normalizes it.

```js
// Trust only the first IP when behind a single proxy (nginx/Cloudflare)
app.set('trust proxy', 1);
const ip = req.ip; // normalized by Express
```

### 2. Email Address (normalized)
- **Pros:** Semantic identity; users don't expect to submit twice with the same email
- **Cons:** Disposable email services, subaddressing (`user+1@gmail.com`)
- **Normalization required:**
  - Lowercase the domain
  - Strip `+tag` subaddressing for Gmail/Outlook
  - Normalize dots in Gmail local parts (`j.o.h.n` → `john`)

```js
function normalizeEmail(raw) {
  const [local, domain] = raw.toLowerCase().split('@');
  if (domain === 'gmail.com' || domain === 'googlemail.com') {
    return local.replace(/\./g, '').split('+')[0] + '@gmail.com';
  }
  return local.split('+')[0] + '@' + domain;
}
```

### 3. Browser Fingerprint
- **Pros:** Survives IP rotation, persists across sessions
- **Cons:** Not available on server-side alone; requires client-side JS; sophisticated bots can spoof
- **Libraries:**
  - **FingerprintJS Open Source** — free, ~60–70% accuracy, runs in browser
  - **Fingerprint Pro** (`fingerprint.com`) — paid ($99+/mo for 100k identifications), 99.5% accuracy, includes bot detection, fraud signals, VPN detection
- **Pattern:** Generate a `visitorId` on the client, submit it with the form, verify server-side that the token was issued recently by Fingerprint's API

### 4. Honeypot Fields
- A hidden form field (CSS `display:none`, not `type="hidden"`) that legitimate users never fill in
- Bots filling forms programmatically will populate it
- Zero friction for real users, zero server cost

```html
<input type="text" name="_hp_email" tabindex="-1" autocomplete="off"
       style="position:absolute;left:-9999px" aria-hidden="true">
```

```js
if (req.body._hp_email && req.body._hp_email.length > 0) {
  return res.status(400).json({ error: 'Bad request' }); // silent drop preferred
}
```

### 5. Time-on-Page / Submission Timing
- Humans take ≥ 3–5 seconds to fill a form; bots often submit in < 1 second
- Embed a hidden timestamp when the form renders; check elapsed time on submit

```js
// Server renders form with signed timestamp
const token = jwt.sign({ t: Date.now() }, SECRET, { expiresIn: '1h' });
// On submit, verify and check elapsed time
const { t } = jwt.verify(formToken, SECRET);
if (Date.now() - t < 2000) return res.status(429).json({ error: 'Too fast' });
```

### 6. CAPTCHA
- **hCaptcha** — free tier available, privacy-focused, GDPR-friendly
- **Cloudflare Turnstile** — free, invisible/non-intrusive, integrates with CF WAF
- **reCAPTCHA v3** — score-based (0.0–1.0), no user interaction, Google-owned
- **Recommendation:** Turnstile for Cloudflare-hosted apps; hCaptcha otherwise. Avoid v2 checkbox — high friction

---

## Production-Ready Libraries

### `express-rate-limit` (most common)
- **npm:** `express-rate-limit`
- **License:** MIT
- **In-process store by default** (not suitable for multi-instance deployments)
- **External stores:** `rate-limit-redis`, `rate-limit-mongo`, `@express-rate-limit/cluster`

```js
import rateLimit from 'express-rate-limit';
import { RedisStore } from 'rate-limit-redis';
import { createClient } from 'redis';

const redis = createClient({ url: process.env.REDIS_URL });
await redis.connect();

export const entryLimiter = rateLimit({
  windowMs: 60 * 60 * 1000,   // 1 hour
  max: 3,                      // 3 attempts per IP per hour
  standardHeaders: 'draft-7',  // Return RateLimit headers
  legacyHeaders: false,
  store: new RedisStore({ sendCommand: (...args) => redis.sendCommand(args) }),
  keyGenerator: (req) => req.ip, // customize per signal
  handler: (req, res) => {
    res.status(429).json({
      error: 'Too many submissions. Try again later.',
      retryAfter: Math.ceil(req.rateLimit.resetTime / 1000),
    });
  },
});
```

### `@upstash/ratelimit` (serverless-native)
- **npm:** `@upstash/ratelimit`
- **Best for:** Vercel Edge, Cloudflare Workers, AWS Lambda — no persistent connections
- **Backend:** Upstash Redis (free tier: 10k commands/day; pay-as-you-go $0.20/100k commands)
- **Algorithms:** Fixed window, sliding window, token bucket, cached fixed window

```js
import { Ratelimit } from '@upstash/ratelimit';
import { Redis } from '@upstash/redis';

const ratelimit = new Ratelimit({
  redis: Redis.fromEnv(),
  limiter: Ratelimit.slidingWindow(5, '1 h'),
  analytics: true,      // tracks usage in Upstash console
  prefix: 'entry:ip',
});

// In your handler:
const { success, limit, remaining, reset } = await ratelimit.limit(req.ip);
if (!success) {
  return res.status(429).json({ error: 'Rate limit exceeded', reset });
}
```

**Multi-signal limiting with Upstash:**
```js
const [ipResult, emailResult] = await Promise.all([
  ipLimiter.limit(req.ip),
  emailLimiter.limit(normalizeEmail(req.body.email)),
]);
if (!ipResult.success || !emailResult.success) {
  return res.status(429).json({ error: 'Submission limit reached' });
}
```

### `bottleneck` (programmatic, not middleware-based)
- Useful when you want to queue/throttle outgoing operations (e.g., email sends after entry)
- Not designed for HTTP rate limiting gates

### `node-rate-limiter-flexible`
- **npm:** `rate-limiter-flexible`
- Most feature-rich Node.js rate limiting library
- Supports Redis, Mongo, Memory, Cluster, DynamoDB
- Point-based consumption (different actions cost different amounts)
- Insurance limiter pattern (in-memory fallback if Redis is down)

```js
import { RateLimiterRedis } from 'rate-limiter-flexible';

const limiter = new RateLimiterRedis({
  storeClient: redisClient,
  keyPrefix: 'entry_ip',
  points: 3,           // 3 submissions
  duration: 3600,      // per hour
  blockDuration: 3600, // block for 1 hour after exhaustion
});

try {
  await limiter.consume(req.ip);
} catch (rej) {
  res.set('Retry-After', Math.ceil(rej.msBeforeNext / 1000));
  return res.status(429).json({ error: 'Too many requests' });
}
```

**Comparison table:**

| Library | Multi-instance | Algorithm | Serverless | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `express-rate-limit` | Yes (with store) | Fixed/Sliding window | Limited | Low |
| `@upstash/ratelimit` | Yes (Upstash) | Fixed/Sliding/Token bucket | Native | Low |
| `rate-limiter-flexible` | Yes (Redis/Mongo) | Token bucket, Fixed, Sliding | Yes | Medium |

---

## Layered Defense Architecture

The most resilient approach applies multiple independent layers. Any layer can reject independently:

```
Request → [1. WAF/CDN] → [2. Honeypot] → [3. CAPTCHA token verify] → [4. IP rate limit]
        → [5. Email rate limit] → [6. Fingerprint check] → [7. Timing check]
        → [8. Duplicate detection] → Submit to DB
```

### Layer 1: CDN/WAF (before your app)
- **Cloudflare WAF** — free tier; rate limit rules by IP, path, country; bot detection
- **AWS WAF** — $5/mo + $1/million requests; integrates with ALB/API Gateway
- Set aggressive limits here (e.g., > 20 requests/minute/IP to your endpoint = auto-block 1h)

### Layer 2: Honeypot (zero cost)
Described above. Always include. Catches form-fill bots with zero user friction.

### Layer 3: CAPTCHA token verification
Validate the CAPTCHA server-side before any rate limit check (prevents token replays):

```js
async function verifyCfTurnstile(token, ip) {
  const res = await fetch('https://challenges.cloudflare.com/turnstile/v0/siteverify', {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
    body: JSON.stringify({
      secret: process.env.TURNSTILE_SECRET,
      response: token,
      remoteip: ip,
    }),
  });
  const data = await res.json();
  return data.success === true;
}
```

### Layer 4 & 5: IP + Email Rate Limiting
Use sliding window for better protection against boundary gaming:
- IP: 3 submissions / hour, 10 / day
- Email (normalized): 1 submission lifetime (or 1/contest period)

### Layer 6: Fingerprint deduplication
Store `visitorId` in your submissions table; reject duplicates:

```js
const existing = await db.query(
  'SELECT id FROM entries WHERE fingerprint_id = $1 AND contest_id = $2',
  [visitorId, contestId]
);
if (existing.rows.length > 0) {
  return res.status(409).json({ error: 'Already entered' });
}
```

### Layer 7: Timing check
Reject submissions that arrive faster than humanly possible (< 2–3 seconds after form render).

### Layer 8: Duplicate detection in DB
Idempotency key or unique constraint on `(email_normalized, contest_id)` as the final backstop — even if all other layers are bypassed, the DB constraint prevents duplicate records.

---

## Redis Key Design

Use structured prefixes with TTL to avoid unbounded growth:

```
entry:ip:{ip}:{contest_id}           TTL = window duration
entry:email:{normalized_email}:{contest_id}   TTL = contest duration
entry:fp:{visitor_id}:{contest_id}   TTL = contest duration
entry:block:{ip}                     TTL = block duration
```

Using contest-scoped keys means limits reset automatically for each new contest without manual cleanup.

---

## Response Strategy: What to Return

**Never reveal which signal triggered the block.** Return a generic message:

```js
// Good
res.status(429).json({ error: 'Submission limit reached. Please try again later.' });

// Bad — tells attacker which signal to rotate
res.status(429).json({ error: 'This IP address has been blocked.' });
```

**Silent drops for honeypot hits:**
Return `200 OK` with a fake success message. Bots don't retry on success; humans never see the honeypot field.

---

## Monitoring & Alerting

Track these metrics to detect active abuse campaigns:

| Metric | Alert threshold |
|---|---|
| `entry.rejected.ip_limit` per minute | > 50 in 5min window |
| `entry.rejected.honeypot` per minute | > 10 in 5min window |
| Unique IPs submitting per minute | > 30 |
| Submissions from ASNs in `datacenter` category | > 5% of total |

Upstash Analytics, Datadog, or a simple Redis `INCR` + CloudWatch metric filter all work.

---

## Disposable Email Detection

For contests where email uniqueness matters, add a disposable-email check:

- **`disposable-email-domains`** npm package — static blocklist, ~100k domains, updated regularly
- **Abstract API** (`abstractapi.com/email-verification`) — $9/mo for 100k verifications; returns `is_disposable`, `is_free_email`, MX record check
- **Mailcheck.ai** — free for low volume; API returns `disposable: true/false`

```js
import disposableDomains from 'disposable-email-domains';

function isDisposable(email) {
  const domain = email.split('@')[1]?.toLowerCase();
  return disposableDomains.includes(domain);
}
```

---

## Implementation Checklist

- [ ] `trust proxy` set correctly for your deployment (Cloudflare → `1`, no proxy → `false`)
- [ ] Rate limits backed by Redis (not in-memory) — safe for multi-instance deployments
- [ ] Sliding window algorithm preferred over fixed window (no burst at window boundary)
- [ ] IP limit **and** email limit applied independently (both must pass)
- [ ] Email normalized before hashing/storing
- [ ] Unique DB constraint on `(email_normalized, contest_id)` as final backstop
- [ ] Honeypot field present in rendered form HTML
- [ ] Submission timing check (reject < 2s)
- [ ] CAPTCHA verified server-side (never trust client-side only)
- [ ] Disposable email check if email uniqueness is a contest rule
- [ ] Generic error messages (no signal disclosure)
- [ ] Rate limit response headers returned (`RateLimit-Remaining`, `Retry-After`)
- [ ] Alerting on spike in rejected submissions

---

## Recommended Stack for a Node.js Contest Entry Endpoint

| Concern | Recommendation |
|---|---|
| Serverless/Edge | `@upstash/ratelimit` + Upstash Redis |
| Traditional server | `rate-limiter-flexible` + Redis |
| CDN-level protection | Cloudflare WAF rate limit rules |
| CAPTCHA | Cloudflare Turnstile (free) or hCaptcha |
| Fingerprinting | Fingerprint Pro if budget allows; FingerprintJS OSS otherwise |
| Disposable email | `disposable-email-domains` npm package |
| Duplicate backstop | DB unique constraint on normalized email + contest ID |
